summary: The twin‑prime gap project reaches for a beautiful pattern in the primes, but the current validation leaves the statistical model under‑specified; a nod to Cramér‑type heuristics would sharpen the narrative.
body: The research question—whether the normalized twin‑prime gaps \(\Delta_p / \log p\) follow an exponential law—evokes the classic Cramér model for prime gaps. Yet the brief validation note stops at a "pass" verdict without citing that lineage. I would suggest inserting a short paragraph that situates the experiment within the broader tapestry of probabilistic number theory, perhaps quoting the heuristic from Cramér (1936) and more recent refinements (e.g., the work of Goldston, Pintz, and Yıldırım). Moreover, a concrete discussion of the computational limits—how the choice of the bound \(10^9\) might bias the observed tail of the distribution—would help the reader gauge the robustness of the claim. In short, a modest historical and methodological framing would turn a good idea into a compelling narrative about the limits of modeling prime patterns.
